text: The robotic surgical setup is visible, with a fourth arm in use. The surgeon examines the area around the previous mesh, which is a large 3D Max mid mesh placed too high, contributing to the recurrence of the hernia. The mesh is located in the preperitoneal space, and the surgeon attempts to identify a plane between the peritoneum and the mesh, noting the ease of dissection in the retro-rectal space.

The surgeon identifies a folded mesh protruding from the surgical field. The dissection proceeds smoothly in the anterior plane, avoiding injury to the inferior epigastric vessels with careful downward traction. The surgeon uses a fourth robotic arm for retraction, creating a wide open working space while hugging the mesh to prevent injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects anterior to the mesh, ensuring the bladder remains dorsal to avoid injury. Using tension and counter-tension, tissue layers are divided with attention to hemostasis. The surgeon has successfully freed the inferior epigastric vessels and proceeds deeper, targeting the lateral portion where the mesh coverage is insufficient. The blue line on the mesh indicates its distal extent, guiding the surgeon to work broadly with soft tissue while cautiously navigating near the bladder and areolar tissue.

The surgeon moves from a deeper dissection to a more superficial area, focusing on the anterior portion of the mesh. Using cautery, the surgeon aggressively works along a broad plane, avoiding injury to the inferior epigastric vessels. The blue line on the mesh indicates the extent of mesh remaining for dissection. The surgeon identifies a plane containing testicular vessel fat and peritoneum, peeling fatty tissue off the peritoneum. The posterior lateral edge of the mesh is visible, with a microfacial layer protecting the testicular vessels within retroperitoneal fat, allowing the surgeon to begin separating the mesh from this layer.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fatty tissue from the peritoneum, following the edge of the mesh. The mesh is lifted off, revealing a benign plane between the mesh and the testicular vessels. The surgeon is cautious of the vas deferens and testicular vessels, ensuring they are not injured during the dissection. The mesh is successfully peeled off, allowing for potential removal of a significant portion without damaging underlying tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects the sticky mesh from the surrounding tissue, creating a small hole in the peritoneum. The goal is to separate the mesh from the inferior epigastric vessels, vas deferens, and iliac vein. The surgeon cautiously navigates around the bottom edge of the mesh, ensuring not to injure the vas deferens, while utilizing hemostatic techniques to maintain clear visualization. The surrounding tissue appears soft and easy to work with, facilitating the separation of the peritoneum from the mesh.

The surgeon navigates through healthy fatty tissue, extending the opening medially to improve retraction. The dissection progresses into the right retro-rectal space, revealing scar tissue from previous surgery. The mesh is noted to have crossed the midline and is positioned deeply into the retropubic space. An indirect hernia sac is visible, with the fourth arm providing traction to assist in scoring and separating tissue layers.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior pubic ramus, using scissors to feel the bony structure and staying close to it. A visible areolar tissue trail is found, and the mesh is released from Cooper's ligament. The mesh has been peeled away from most critical structures, with potential adhesion to the vas deferens and bladder remaining. The surgeon traces the peritoneum, identifying its distal edge and peeling away any fat adhering to it, using traction and counter traction to define the peritoneum's edge.

The surgeon navigates a mass of fatty tissue medially, where the vas deferens is expected to be located, while exploring the lateral tissue. A vessel crosses the field, herniating through the internal ring into the inguinal canal. The surgeon peels back the scarred peritoneum, revealing its edge, and requests Firefly imaging to identify the testicular vessels and vas deferens. Despite the scarring, the plane is visible, with abundant fat on the opposite side, reducing the risk of injuring the testicular vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects the posterior part of the mesh, separating it from the bladder. The mesh is mobilized, revealing the bladder underneath, but the tissue becomes sticky, limiting further separation. Two tubular structures are observed, one potentially being the left medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon decides to transect the mesh, removing the lateral half while leaving the medial half attached to the bladder and peritoneum.

The surgeon uses scissors and monopolar energy to divide the mesh, carefully avoiding concentrated heat spots. The mesh is too large to remove through an eight-millimeter port, so it is orange peeled for extraction. The surgeon explores the area around the bladder and inferior epigastric vessels, checking for the vas deferens. Fatty tissue is divided, and the peritoneum is peeled back to prevent it from slipping under the new mesh. ICG is used to illuminate the testicular vessels, making the vas deferens and its surrounding vessels visible.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and begins to address the herniated fat through the internal ring. Using Firefly imaging, the surgeon distinguishes the testicular vessels and carefully dissects the surrounding thick fascial-like tissue. The fat is separated from the testicular vessels using tension and counter-tension techniques, ensuring a clear plane for mesh placement.

The surgeon utilizes all four robotic arms, including the scope and grasper, to position a 16x12 ProGrip flat sheet mesh into the retro pubic space. The mesh is trimmed at the upper outer corner for a better fit and tucked behind retroperitoneal fat. The mesh is secured with sutures to Cooper's ligament and near the femoral space due to the large defect. The flap is closed using two 6-inch sutures, and a suction cannula is inserted to evacuate the pocket, ensuring the mesh lays flat.
